subject: Chemistry
chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes; Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers; Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids

questions.question: You are given three colourless bottles containing 1-bromopropane, 2-bromopropane and tert-butyl bromide. If each is subjected to an SN1 reaction under identical conditions, which order correctly represents their decreasing reactivity?
questions.explanation: SN1 reactivity follows carbocation stability: tertiary > secondary > primary.

questions.question: Suppose you wish to convert bromoethane into ethanol in a single step with maximum yield. Which reagent would you choose?
questions.explanation: Aqueous KOH favours nucleophilic substitution, replacing bromine with hydroxyl.

questions.question: A student wants to prepare fluoroethane from bromoethane using a named reaction. Which reagent is most suitable?
questions.explanation: Swarts reaction employs metallic fluorides such as AgF to prepare alkyl fluorides.

questions.question: Read carefully before answering. Which reagent converts an alkyl chloride into the corresponding alkyl iodide through the Finkelstein reaction?
questions.explanation: NaI in dry acetone is used in the Finkelstein reaction.

questions.question: Three compounds are placed on your bench: chlorobenzene, tert-butyl chloride and ethyl chloride. Which option gives their increasing ease of nucleophilic substitution?
questions.explanation: Haloarenes are least reactive. Tertiary alkyl halides undergo substitution most readily through SN1.

questions.question: Many students know chlorobenzene reacts much more slowly than chloroethane towards nucleophilic substitution. What is the best explanation?
options: The chlorine atom is positively charged.
The C-Cl bond acquires partial double-bond character due to resonance.
The benzene ring donates hydroxide ions.
The molecule contains fewer hydrogen atoms.
questions.explanation: Resonance gives the C-Cl bond partial double-bond character, making cleavage difficult.

questions.question: Imagine you accidentally picked alcoholic KOH instead of aqueous KOH while reacting with bromoethane. Which major product would now be obtained?
questions.explanation: Alcoholic KOH favours β-elimination to produce ethene.

questions.question: Without arranging the compounds mentally first, choose the correct increasing order of boiling point for ethanol, ethane and chloroethane.
questions.explanation: Alcohols show hydrogen bonding, haloalkanes have dipole-dipole interactions and alkanes possess only weak dispersion forces.

questions.question: A Grignard reagent prepared from methyl bromide is treated with carbon dioxide followed by acidic hydrolysis. What is the final product?
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents react with CO2 to give carboxylic acids containing one extra carbon atom.

questions.question: Two students use different cyanide reagents with an alkyl halide. One obtains a nitrile while the other obtains an isocyanide. Which reagent leads to isocyanide formation?
questions.explanation: AgCN is largely covalent and attacks through nitrogen, giving isocyanides.

questions.question: You need to prepare an alkyl chloride from ethanol while keeping purification as simple as possible. Which reagent is the best choice?
questions.explanation: SOCl₂ forms alkyl chloride along with gaseous SO₂ and HCl, making purification easier.

questions.question: Don't be fooled by the order below. Which option correctly represents the decreasing boiling point of propane, ethanol and dimethyl ether?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding makes alcohols boil at much higher temperatures than ethers and alkanes.

questions.question: Suppose equal moles of ethanol, butan-1-ol and propan-1-ol are added separately to water. Which option gives the increasing order of their solubility?
questions.explanation: Water solubility decreases as the alkyl chain length increases.

questions.question: One of these alcohols resists oxidation by acidified KMnO₄ under ordinary conditions. Which one is it?
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols lack an α-hydrogen and therefore resist oxidation under ordinary conditions.

questions.question: Phenol is treated with bromine water without using any catalyst. What is the major product?
questions.explanation: Phenol readily forms white precipitates of 2,4,6-tribromophenol.

questions.question: Three alcohols—propan-2-ol, ethanol and tert-butyl alcohol—are tested with Lucas reagent. Which is the correct decreasing order of reactivity?
questions.explanation: Lucas reagent reacts fastest with tertiary alcohols followed by secondary and then primary alcohols.

questions.question: A student wants to convert sodium phenoxide into salicylic acid using a named reaction. Which reaction should be chosen?
questions.explanation: Kolbe reaction converts sodium phenoxide into salicylic acid after CO₂ treatment and acidification.

questions.question: In the Reimer-Tiemann reaction, which combination of reagents is actually used?
questions.explanation: Reimer-Tiemann reaction employs chloroform and aqueous sodium hydroxide.

questions.question: Think carefully before choosing. Which option correctly gives the increasing acidity of water, phenol and ethanol?
questions.explanation: Phenol is most acidic due to resonance stabilization of phenoxide ion, while ethanol is the least acidic.

questions.question: Why is phenol considerably more acidic than ethanol, even though both contain an –OH group?
options: Ethanol has a higher molecular mass.
Phenol contains a benzene ring only.
questions.explanation: The conjugate base of phenol is stabilized by resonance, making proton loss easier.

questions.question: A chemist oxidises propan-2-ol using acidified potassium dichromate. Which compound should be expected as the major product?
questions.explanation: Secondary alcohols undergo oxidation to form ketones. Hence propan-2-ol gives propanone.

questions.question: Don't rely on the order shown. Which option correctly represents the decreasing boiling point of 2-methylpropan-2-ol, propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol?
questions.explanation: Branching lowers boiling point because it decreases surface area available for intermolecular attraction.

questions.question: You need to oxidise ethanol only up to ethanal without significant further oxidation. Which reagent is most suitable?
questions.explanation: PCC selectively oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes.

questions.question: When ethanol is treated with PCC under anhydrous conditions, what is the expected product?
questions.explanation: PCC stops oxidation at the aldehyde stage.

questions.question: A laboratory wants to prepare an aldehyde directly from an acid chloride without reducing it all the way to an alcohol. Which reagent should be selected?
questions.explanation: Rosenmund reduction converts acid chlorides into aldehydes using poisoned palladium catalyst.

questions.question: Which named reaction is specifically used for preparing aldehydes from acid chlorides?
questions.explanation: Rosenmund reduction is the selective reduction of acid chlorides to aldehydes.

questions.question: A nitrile has to be converted into an aldehyde by partial reduction followed by hydrolysis. Which reagent best accomplishes this?
questions.explanation: DIBAL-H partially reduces nitriles to imines, which yield aldehydes after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Read the question carefully. Which reagent is actually employed in the Rosenmund reduction?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen in the presence of poisoned Pd supported on BaSO₄ is used in Rosenmund reduction.

questions.question: Under carefully controlled oxidation conditions, propan-1-ol gives which major product?
questions.explanation: Controlled oxidation converts a primary alcohol first into the corresponding aldehyde.

questions.question: Test your understanding rather than memorising. Which option correctly gives the increasing ease of oxidation of propan-2-ol, propan-1-ol and 2-methylpropan-2-ol?
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation, secondary alcohols oxidise less readily than primary alcohols.

questions.question: Which alcohol produces acetone when oxidised with acidified potassium dichromate?
questions.explanation: Propan-2-ol is a secondary alcohol and oxidises to propanone (acetone).

questions.question: A secondary alcohol has to be converted into the corresponding ketone. Which reagent below is the most suitable?
questions.explanation: Acidified potassium dichromate readily oxidises secondary alcohols into ketones.

questions.question: Which reagent converts an acid chloride into a ketone without further addition to the carbonyl carbon?
questions.explanation: Dialkylcadmium reagents react with acid chlorides to produce ketones.

questions.question: When ethanoyl chloride reacts with dimethylcadmium, what is the major organic product?
questions.explanation: Dimethylcadmium reacts with ethanoyl chloride to produce propanone.

questions.question: Benzonitrile is partially reduced using DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis. Which compound is obtained?
questions.explanation: DIBAL-H selectively converts nitriles into aldehydes after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Without rearranging the names yourself first, identify the correct increasing ease of oxidation of tert-butyl alcohol, ethanol and propan-2-ol.
questions.explanation: Primary alcohols oxidise most readily, followed by secondary alcohols, while tertiary alcohols resist oxidation.

questions.question: A nitrile is converted into an aldehyde using SnCl₂/HCl followed by hydrolysis. Which named reaction is being used?
questions.explanation: Stephen reaction converts nitriles into aldehydes via an iminium salt intermediate.

questions.question: The Stephen reaction starts from which functional group?
questions.explanation: Stephen reduction converts nitriles into aldehydes after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Propan-2-ol is treated with acidified KMnO₄. Which compound is expected as the major product?
questions.explanation: Secondary alcohols are oxidised into ketones by acidified potassium permanganate.

questions.question: If your goal is to stop oxidation at the aldehyde stage, which reagent below should generally NOT be selected?
questions.explanation: Acidified KMnO₄ is a strong oxidising agent that usually oxidises aldehydes further into carboxylic acids.

questions.question: A sample of propan-1-ol is oxidised gently with PCC under anhydrous conditions. Which compound is obtained as the major product?
questions.explanation: PCC selectively oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes without significant further oxidation.

questions.question: Look carefully before choosing. Which option correctly represents the increasing ease of oxidation of methanol, tert-butyl alcohol and propan-2-ol?
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols are most resistant to oxidation, secondary alcohols oxidise less readily than primary alcohols.

questions.question: An alkyne is hydrated in the presence of dilute H₂SO₄ and HgSO₄. Which reagent combination shown below is actually used in the NCERT method?
questions.explanation: Hydration of alkynes is carried out using dilute sulphuric acid in the presence of mercuric sulphate catalyst.

questions.question: Propyne is hydrated using HgSO₄ and dilute H₂SO₄. After tautomerisation, which compound is finally obtained?
questions.explanation: Hydration first forms an enol, which rapidly tautomerises to propanone.

questions.question: During hydration of an alkyne, which intermediate is actually formed before the stable carbonyl compound appears?
questions.explanation: The reaction initially produces an enol, which undergoes keto-enol tautomerisation.

questions.question: The conversion of an enol into its corresponding aldehyde or ketone is known as:
questions.explanation: Keto-enol tautomerisation converts the unstable enol into the more stable carbonyl compound.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why the carbonyl compound is obtained after hydration of an alkyne instead of isolating the enol?
options: The enol is more stable than the keto form.
Mercuric sulphate directly forms the ketone without any intermediate.
The initially formed enol rapidly undergoes keto-enol tautomerisation.
Water behaves as a reducing agent.
The reaction follows an SN1 mechanism.
The alkyne is first converted into a carboxylic acid.
questions.explanation: The enol intermediate spontaneously rearranges to the thermodynamically more stable keto form.

questions.question: Which compound gives propanone after hydration followed by keto-enol tautomerisation?
questions.explanation: Hydration of propyne produces an enol that rearranges to propanone.

questions.question: A compound gives a yellow precipitate with iodine and sodium hydroxide in the haloform test. According to the NCERT discussion up to this point, what does this most strongly suggest?
options: It contains only a carboxyl group.
It is necessarily an aromatic aldehyde.
It contains a methyl ketone group or can be oxidised to one.
It is always a tertiary alcohol.
questions.explanation: The haloform reaction is characteristic of methyl ketones and compounds that are readily oxidised to methyl ketones.

questions.question: Which of the following compounds is expected to undergo the haloform reaction most directly?
questions.explanation: Propanone is a methyl ketone and readily undergoes the haloform reaction to produce iodoform (yellow precipitate) with I₂/NaOH.
